3	\chapter{The rational-curve isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ (RR.4)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1 Example~1.3.5
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
8	% + IV \S2, opening of "Hurwitz's Theorem" (degree of a finite morphism of
9	% curves is the function-field extension degree; read from PDF page 316)
10	% + I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field $\Leftrightarrow$ smooth-projective-curve
11	% equivalence of categories; read from PDF page 62). The verbatim quotes
12	% pasted in the declaration blocks below were copied character-by-character
13	% from the rendered page images of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
18	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
26	\begin{itemize}
27	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
28	    group $\Div(C)$, the degree map
29	    $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ (\Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
30	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
31	    (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
32	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
33	    formula in genus zero $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$
34	    (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
35	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
36	    the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$, its global-sections identification
37	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2$, and the corollary
38	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
39	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
40	    \bar k \cdot 1$ on a genus-$0$ curve with a marked $\bar k$-point.
41	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
42	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$
43	    and a $\bar k$-rational point is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.''
44	\end{itemize}
45	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
46	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
47	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
48	the interface block \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
49	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
50	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
51	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
52	\Cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
53	
54	\paragraph{Scope of the $\bar k$-base classification.} The chapter restricts
55	to the case where the base field $\bar k$ is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
56	k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so the ``$C$ has a $\bar
57	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
58	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ is non-empty,
59	hence has closed points, all of which are $\bar k$-rational). The general
60	``$k \to \bar k$ descent'' step ``genus-$0$ curve over a non-closed $k$ with
61	a $k$-rational point $\cong \mathbb P^1_k$'' is a separate sub-build hosted
62	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
63	consumer) and is out of scope here.
64	
65	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
66	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
67	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. The Lean signature
68	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
69	project's standing curve typeclass package:
70	\begin{quote}
71	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
72	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
73	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
74	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
75	\end{quote}
76	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
77	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
78	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
79	$g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
80	namely $g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ via the project's
81	$\Module \bar k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology
82	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
83	$\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is the project's
84	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of
85	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean}.
86	
87	\section{The morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from two global sections}
88	
89	\begin{lemma}
90	[Morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from a globally generating pair of sections]
91	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
92	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
93	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
94	  Let $X$ be a scheme over a field $\bar k$, let $\mathcal L$ be an invertible
95	  sheaf on $X$, and let $s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)$ be two global
96	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
97	  \[
98	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
99	  \]
100	  (Equivalently, $s_0$ and $s_1$ generate $\mathcal L$ at every point.) Then
101	  there exists a unique morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
102	  \[
103	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
104	  \]
105	  whose pullback of $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)$ is $\mathcal L$ and whose
106	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
107	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))$ are $s_0$ and $s_1$ respectively. On the
108	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
109	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ the morphism $\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}$ is the map ``$x
110	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)$''; on the chart $D_+(x_1)$ it is the map ``$x
111	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
112	  $D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}$ by the cocycle identity
113	  $(s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1$.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{proof}
117	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
118	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
119	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to $\mathbb P^n$''), specialised to
120	  $n = 1$. Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
121	  under the name
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
123	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
124	
125	  \begin{quote}
126	  \small
127	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{$\sigma$ A\} [CommRing A]
128	    [SetLike $\sigma$ A] [AddSubgroupClass $\sigma$ A] ($\mathcal A$ :
129	    \(\mathbb N\) → $\sigma$) [GradedRing $\mathcal A$] \{X : Scheme\}\\
130	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj $\top$)\\
131	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant $\mathcal A$).toIdeal = $\top$) :\\
132	    \phantom{}\quad(X $\longrightarrow$ Proj $\mathcal A$)}
133	  \end{quote}
134	
135	  Specialising to $\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ with the standard $\mathbb
136	  N$-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded $\bar k$-algebra
137	  homomorphism $f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and a
138	  proof that the irrelevant ideal $(x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ maps to
139	  the unit ideal of $H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
140	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
141	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of $\mathbb
142	  P^n$ as a $\mathrm{Proj}$). When $\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
143	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
144	  cover $X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)$, where on each chart the sections become
145	  honest global functions ($s_1 / s_0$ on $D(s_0)$, $s_0 / s_1$ on $D(s_1)$,
146	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
147	  project-bespoke wrapper
148	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
149	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
150	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
151	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
152	
153	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
154	  a separated scheme: two morphisms $\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1$
155	  with $\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i$ for $i = 0, 1$ agree on the basic-open
156	  charts $D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)$ (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
157	  the same rational function), and these charts cover $\mathbb P^1$, so the
158	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
159	\end{proof}
160	
161	\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}
162	
163	\begin{lemma}
164	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
165	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
168	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
169	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
170	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
171	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
172	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
173	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
175	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
176	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
177	  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
178	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ is finite, and the degree of
179	  $\varphi$
180	  \[
181	    \deg(\varphi) \;:=\; [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] \;\in\; \mathbb N_{>0}
182	  \]
183	  is recovered as the degree of the pullback of any closed point of $\mathbb
184	  P^1$: for every closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$,
185	  \[
186	    \deg(\varphi^*[Q]) \;=\; \deg(\varphi).
187	  \]
188	  In particular, taking $Q = \infty \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the morphism
189	  satisfies
190	  \[
191	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\;
192	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
193	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
194	  \]
195	  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
196	  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
197	  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$).
198	\end{lemma}
199	
200	\begin{proof}
201	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point,
202	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
203	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$ is
204	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of $\varphi$ is a closed
205	  irreducible subset of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ that is not a point (else
206	  $\varphi$ would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
207	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (the only proper irreducible closed subset of $\mathbb
208	  P^1_{\bar k}$ is a point); hence $\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
209	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
210	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
211	  fact that $\varphi$ has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
212	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
213	  contradicting irreducibility of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $C$ at
214	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
215	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$
216	  is finite.
217	
218	  The identification $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$ for
219	  any closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ is the multiplicativity of the
220	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
221	  $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
222	  \Div(C')$, $\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
223	  to $D = [Q]$ (a divisor of degree $1$ since $\kappa(Q) = \bar k$) gives
224	  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)$. Equivalently, the degree of
225	  $\varphi$ equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
226	  P^1$, counted with the ramification multiplicities $\ord_P(\varphi^*
227	  t_\infty)$ at the preimage points $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
228	
229	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
230	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
231	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
232	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
233	  and the multiplicativity of $\deg$ under composition of $\div$ and
234	  $\deg$; the closely related ``$\div(f)$ has degree zero'' specialisation
235	  is \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
236	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
237	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
238	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
239	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
240	\end{proof}
241	
242	\section{A degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}
245	[Degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
246	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
248	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
249	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
250	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
251	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
252	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
253	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
255	  % equivalent:}
256	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
257	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
258	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over $k$, and $k$-homomorphisms.}"
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
260	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
261	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
262	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
263	  (p.~299).}
264	  Let $C, C'$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
265	  algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ be
266	  a non-constant morphism with $\deg(\varphi) = 1$. Then $\varphi$ is an
267	  isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
268	\end{lemma}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	  By \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} (specialised to a non-constant
272	  morphism, not necessarily to $\mathbb P^1$; the same argument applies to
273	  any non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$),
274	  the morphism $\varphi$ is finite and the function-field extension
275	  $\varphi^\# \colon \bar k(C') \hookrightarrow \bar k(C)$ has degree
276	  $[\bar k(C) : \bar k(C')] = \deg(\varphi) = 1$. Therefore $\varphi^\#$
277	  is an isomorphism of function fields $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$.
278	
279	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment $C \mapsto \bar k(C)$ is
280	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
281	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
282	  one over $\bar k$ (with $\bar k$-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
283	  the isomorphism $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$ produced by the previous
284	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
285	  C'$, and this isomorphism agrees with $\varphi$ on the generic point (by
286	  construction: it is the inverse-image of $\varphi^\#$ under the
287	  equivalence). The two morphisms $C \to C'$ that agree on the generic
288	  point of the integral scheme $C$ agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
289	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
290	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence $\varphi$ is itself
291	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
292	
293	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
294	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
295	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-$1$ morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
296	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
297	  Indeed, the direct image $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C$ is a coherent
298	  $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
299	  1$, and an inclusion $\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
300	  \mathcal O_C$ of coherent rank-$1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
301	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
302	  $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}$ has trivial stalk. The
303	  quotient is a torsion coherent $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module of generic rank
304	  $\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0$, hence is itself torsion-free (a
305	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
306	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
307	  \mathcal O_{C'}$ and $\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
308	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
309	
310	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
312	  non-constant finite morphism $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ together with the
313	  degree-$1$ hypothesis $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ and produces an isomorphism
314	  $C \cong C'$ in the slice category $\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}$.
315	  The proof body threads the Mathlib API
316	  \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
317	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
318	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
319	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
320	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
321	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
322	\end{proof}

324	\section{Headline: genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
325	
326	\begin{theorem}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$]
330	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
332	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
333	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
334	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
335	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
336	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
337	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S1 Example~1.3.5 (genus-zero classification;
338	  % "rational $\iff g = 0$" + "rational curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ for
339	  % complete nonsingular curves") (read from
340	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314, doc p.~297).
341	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
342	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this
343	  % chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve $X$ is
344	  % rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now using
345	  % (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We
346	  % already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
347	  % suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two distinct
348	  % points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor $D = P - Q$.
349	  % Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$,
350	  % and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by
351	  % (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies
352	  % $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
353	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
354	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, and let $C$ be a smooth
355	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$.
356	  Then there exists an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes
357	  \[
358	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
359	  \]
360	  Concretely, the Lean signature
361	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
362	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
363	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
364	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
365	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is its consumer-side restatement,
366	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
370	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
371	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
372	% "We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots"). The present
373	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
374	% (linear-system construction $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
375	% ([P]))$, degree-$1$ identification, isomorphism).
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
378	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
379	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
380	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
381	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
382	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ the smooth proper
383	  geometrically irreducible curve $C$ has closed points, all of which are
384	  $\bar k$-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
385	  $\bar k$ is a finite extension of $\bar k$, hence is $\bar k$ itself by
386	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such $\bar k$-rational point $P \in C$;
387	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
388	
389	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$.}
390	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
391	  (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
392	  effective divisor $[P]$ of degree $1$, we obtain
393	  $\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2$. The constant section
394	  $1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ accounts for a
395	  one-dimensional $\bar k$-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
396	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
397	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
398	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
399	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
400	
401	  \emph{Step 2: morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via the linear system $(1, f)$.}
402	  The pair $(1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2$ generates the
403	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ at every point of $C$: at points
404	  $Q \in C \setminus \{P\}$ the section $1$ does not vanish in
405	  $\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}$, and at the marked point
406	  $P$ the section $f$ does not vanish in $\mathcal O_C([P])_P$ (because $f$
407	  has a pole of order exactly $1$ at $P$ in the rational-function
408	  picture: $f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k$ with $\div(f)_\infty = [P]$).
409	  Therefore by \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
410	  exists a unique morphism
411	  \[
412	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
413	  \]
414	  with $\varphi^* x_0 = 1$ and $\varphi^* x_1 = f$ (so the pullback of
415	  the standard ratio coordinate $u = x_1 / x_0$ on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
416	  \{\infty\}$ is $f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
417	  $t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f$ is the local parameter $1/f$ at $P$).
418	
419	  \emph{Step 3: $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ via the pole divisor of $f$.} The
420	  morphism $\varphi$ is non-constant (since $f$ is non-constant as a
421	  rational function on $C$, the pullback $\varphi^* u = f$ takes
422	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant $\varphi$). By
423	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to $\varphi$,
424	  \[
425	    \deg(\varphi)
426	    \;=\;
427	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
428	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
429	  \]
430	  We compute the pole divisor of $f$ as a rational function on $C$:
431	  since $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ with $f$ non-constant and
432	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ the line bundle of the effective divisor $[P]$, the
433	  rational-function $f$ has pole divisor $(f)_\infty = [P]$ (the
434	  unique pole of order $1$ at $P$, no other poles; this is the standard
435	  identification of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$ with the rational functions
436	  $g \in K(C)$ satisfying $\div(g) + D \geq 0$, specialised to
437	  $D = [P]$). Hence $\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}$ with multiplicity~$1$
438	  and
439	  \[
440	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
441	  \]
442	
443	  \emph{Step 4: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism.} By
444	  \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant degree-$1$
445	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (smooth proper
446	  geometrically irreducible curves over $\bar k$ on both sides), $\varphi$
447	  is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
448	
449	  This produces the required isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$
450	  closing the headline target
451	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
452	
453	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
454	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
455	  $P, Q \in C$ and the degree-zero divisor $D = P - Q$, deriving $P \sim Q$
456	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that $C$ is rational; combined with I.6.12
457	  this yields $C \cong \mathbb P^1$. The present proof restates the same
458	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
459	  construction: instead of going through the $P \sim Q$ statement and
460	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
461	  $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1$ from the two sections $(1, f)$ of
462	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
463	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
464	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero $\Rightarrow$
465	  $\ell([P]) = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ non-constant $f$ $\Rightarrow$ degree-$1$
466	  map to $\mathbb P^1$ $\Rightarrow$ iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
467	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
468	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
469	\end{proof}
470

472	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-$0$ Albanese keystone]

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.

99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	[Line bundle of a closed point]
104	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
106	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
107	        def:order_at_point}
108	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
109	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
110	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
111	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
112	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
113	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
114	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
115	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
116	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
117	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
118	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
119	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
120	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
121	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
122	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
123	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
124	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
125	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
126	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
127	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
128	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
129	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
130	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
131	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
132	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
133	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
134	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
135	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
136	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
137	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
138	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
139	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
140	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
141	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
142	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
143	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
144	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
145	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
146	
147	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
148	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
149	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
150	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
151	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
152	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
153	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
156	    \mathcal O_C)
157	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
158	  \]
159	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
160	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
161	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
162	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
163	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
164	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
165	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
169	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
170	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
171	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
172	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
173	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
174	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
175	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
176	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
177	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
178	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
179	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
180	\end{definition}

182	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
187	controlled pole]
188	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
190	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
191	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
192	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
193	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
194	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
195	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
196	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
199	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
200	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
201	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
202	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
203	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
204	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
205	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
206	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
207	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
208	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
209	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
210	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
211	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
213	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
214	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
215	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
216	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
217	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
218	  Riemann--Roch space
219	  \[
220	    L([P]) \;:=\;
221	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
222	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
223	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
224	  \]
225	  Equivalently, $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ defines a global section of
226	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if and only if
227	  \[
228	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
229	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
230	    \qquad
231	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1.
232	  \]
233	  Under this identification, the inclusion
234	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
235	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
236	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
237	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
242	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
243	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
244	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
245	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
246	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
247	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
248	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
249	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
250	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
251	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
252	  and only if:
253	
254	  \begin{itemize}
255	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
256	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
257	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
258	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
259	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
260	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
261	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
262	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
263	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
264	  \end{itemize}
265	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
266	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
267	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
268	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
269	  zero global section.
270	
271	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
272	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
273	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
274	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
275	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
276	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
277	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
278	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
279	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
280	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
281	\end{proof}

283	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
284	
285	\begin{lemma}
286	
287	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
288	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
289	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
290	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
291	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
292	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
293	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
294	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
295	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
296	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
297	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
299	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
300	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
301	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
302	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
303	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
304	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
305	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
306	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
307	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
308	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
309	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
310	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
311	  \[
312	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
313	  \]
314	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
315	\end{lemma}
316	
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
319	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
320	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
321	  exact sequence
322	  \[
323	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
324	  \]
325	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
326	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
327	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
328	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
329	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
330	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
331	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
332	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
333	  \[
334	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
335	  \]
336	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
337	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
338	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
339	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
340	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
341	  \begin{multline*}
342	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
343	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
344	  \end{multline*}
345	  Two ingredients close the proof:
346	
347	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
348	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
349	  \[
350	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
351	  \]
352	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
353	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
354	
355	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
356	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
357	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
358	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
359	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
360	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
361	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
362	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
363	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
364	
365	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
366	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
367	  \[
368	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
369	  \]
370	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
371	
372	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
373	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
374	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
375	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
376	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
377	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
378	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
379	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
380	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
381	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
382	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
383	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
384	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
385	  complex degenerates).
386	\end{proof}

388	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
389	
390	\begin{theorem}
391	
392	[$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
393	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
395	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
396	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
397	        def:eulerChar_curve,
398	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
399	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
400	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
401	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with $D = [P]$: $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
402	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
403	  % $H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0$ (read from
404	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
405	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
406	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
407	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
408	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
409	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
410	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
411	  % is the Euler characteristic
412	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
413	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
414	  $\chi$-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
415	  genus-$0$ specialisation chain).}
416	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
417	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
418	  \[
419	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
420	  \]
421	\end{theorem}
422	
423	\begin{proof}
424	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
425	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
426	        def:eulerChar_curve,
427	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
428	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
429	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
430	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2} to
431	  the divisor $D = [P]$ of \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The
432	  degree of $[P]$ is $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
433	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
434	  genus hypothesis $g(C) = 0$ (\Cref{def:genus}) gives
435	  \[
436	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
437	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
438	  \]
439	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} as a two-term Euler
440	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
441	  \[
442	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
443	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
444	    \;=\; 2.
445	  \]
446	  By
447	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}, the second
448	  term vanishes:
449	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$. Substituting yields
450	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, as required.
451	
452	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
453	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
454	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} computes
455	  the dimension of $H^0$ in one stroke without invoking
456	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
457	  \[
458	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
459	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
460	  \]
461	  together with $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$
462	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: $H^0$ of the structure sheaf on a proper
463	  geometrically irreducible variety over $\bar k$ is $\bar k$),
464	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ (skyscraper at a $\bar k$-rational
465	  point), and $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ (genus-$0$), the connecting
466	  map $H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ has zero codomain and the
467	  segment yields the short exact sequence
468	  \[
469	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
470	  \]
471	  of $\bar k$-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
472	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
473	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
474	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
475	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
476	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
477	\end{proof}

479	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
480	
481	\begin{corollary}
482	
483	[A genus-$0$ curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
484	single simple pole]
485	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
487	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
488	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
489	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
490	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
491	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
492	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
493	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
494	  % point on a genus-$0$ curve, as a direct consequence of the
495	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
496	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
497	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let $X$ be a curve, and let $P \in X$ be a
498	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
499	  % K(X)$, which is regular everywhere except at $P$."
500	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
501	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
502	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
503	  there exists a non-constant rational function $f \in K(C)$ such that:
504	  \begin{itemize}
505	    \item $f$ is regular on $C \setminus \{P\}$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
506	      for every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$;
507	    \item $f$ has at most a simple pole at $P$, i.e.\
508	      $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$;
509	    \item $f \notin \bar k$, equivalently
510	      $\div(f) \neq 0 \in \Div(C)$.
511	  \end{itemize}
512	  In particular, the pair $(1, f)$ spans
513	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
514	\end{corollary}
515	
516	\begin{proof}
517	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
518	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
519	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
520	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
521	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
522	  By
523	  \Cref{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero},
524	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$. The constant subspace
525	  $\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, obtained from the
526	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
527	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
528	  $f = 1$, with $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, under the
529	  identification of
530	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}), is a one-dimensional
531	  $\bar k$-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension $2$,
532	  the quotient $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1$ is a
533	  non-zero one-dimensional $\bar k$-vector space; any lift to
534	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of a non-zero element of the quotient is
535	  an element of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1$.
536	
537	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
538	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
539	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} the section
540	  $s$ corresponds to a rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$
541	  satisfying $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, equivalently $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
542	  for every $Q \neq P$ and $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$. The choice
543	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ means $f$ is not the image of a constant,
544	  so $f \notin \bar k$ (every non-zero element of
545	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k$ corresponds, under the
546	  identification, to a constant element of $K(C)$). The three bullets
547	  of the statement are now immediate.
548
